coredata.dc:title: Effective biosensor based on graphene quantum dots via enzymatic reaction for directly photoluminescence detection of organophosphate pesticide
coredata.dc:creator: Sahub, Chonticha
Tuntulani, Thawatchai
Nhujak, Thumnoon
Tomapatanaget, Boosayarat
coredata.dc:description: In this study, the pesticide sensor by graphene quantum dots (GQDs) and active enzyme, GQDs/enzyme platform, has been developed for monitoring the organophosphate pesticides. In this concept, H2O2 generated from the active enzymatic reaction of acetylcholinesterase (AChE) and choline oxidase (CHOx) enables to react with GQDs resulting in a “turn-off” photoluminescence of GQDs. A “turn-on” photoluminescence of GQDs at 467nm was recovered in the presence of organophosphate. Definitely, the photoluminescence changes of GQDs/AChE/CHOx biosensor reasonably correspond to the amount of pesticide. The detection limit of GQDs/AChE/CHOx biosensor towards dichlorvos was 0.172ppm (0.778μM). In this approach, this biosensor offers the promising determination of the organophosphate pesticides and a benefit for easy checking of organophosphate pesticides in food, water and environment with low cost, easy-to-prepare and less toxic to environment.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene quantum dots
Organophosphate
Photoluminescence
Acetylcholinesterase
Choline oxidase

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of the graphene quantum dots
Preparation of the proposed biosensors and detection of organophosphate
Structural morphology characterizations of GQDs and GQDs/AChE/CHOx
Role of GQDs toward peroxidase-catalytic reaction of H2O2
The GQDs/AChE/CHOx nanomaterial responses to acetylcholine
OPs detection by using the GQDs/AChE/CHOx nanomaterial
Determination of organophosphate in real samples

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Proposed GQDs/AChE/CHOx system to determine the organophosphate pesticide.

The magnification TEM image of GQDs (A), TEM images of GQDs (B) GQDs/AChE/CHOx biosensor (C). Inset of (B) and (C) are the frequency (%) of size of GQDs and GQDs/AChE/CHOx biosensor by TEM image, respectively.
FT-IR of GQDs (in Tris)AChE/CHOx, BSA and GQDs/AChE/CHOx (in Tris and Tris-BSA). The solution samples were freeze-dried before characterization.

(A) UV–vis absorption spectra of the GQDs, inset (A)is the GQDs solution of GQDs observed under visible light. (B)PL spectra of the GQDs with different excitation wavelength (λex 300–440nm) inset (B) is the photoluminescence brightness of GQDs under UV light.

(A) Time-dependent fluorescence changes of 5×10−4 g/mL of the GQDs after interacted with 0.1M H2O2 and (B) Concentration-dependent fluorescence changes at 30min after interacted with different concentration of H2O2.

(A) Time-dependent photoluminescence changes of GQDs/AChE/CHOx after interacted with 1mg/mL of acetylcholine (ACh) in Tris buffer pH 8, the concentration of GQDs, AChE and CHOx are 5×10−4 g/mL, 1U/mL and 0.125U/mL, respectively. (B) Normalized I/I0 photoluminescence responses at λem 467nm of GQDs upon the interaction of various concentrations of AChE and CHOx and incubation time.

(A) Photoluminescence responses of GQDs/AChE/CHOx biosensor after incubation with various concentration of dichlorvos 0–100ppm. (B) % Inhibition efficiency (I%) of 1 and 10ppm of different OPs toward GQDs/AChE/CHOx biosensor.

% Inhibition efficiency (I%) of GQDs/AChE/CHOx biosensor after incubation with various concentration of dichlorvos from 0.025 to 100ppm (A) and methyl-paraoxon from 1×10−5 to 20ppm (B). The insets of (A) and (B) showed the linear rang of dichlorvos detection from 0.1 to 10ppm and methyl-paraoxon from 0.05 to 1ppm, respectively.
Colorimetric of GQDs/AChE/CHOx biosensor in visible (A) and UV light (B) at various concentration of dichlorvos organophosphate (OP) at 0, 1, 10, 100ppm and blank (no dichlorvos and ACh).
Effect of interference (1×10−5 M) to the inhibition efficiency (%) of AChE of biosensor in the presence of dichlorvos (1×10−5 M or 2ppm) after incubation for 30min.
The analytical comparison between this work and other works.
The determination of dichlorvos in real samples by using this biosensor and LC–MS.

head.ce:title: Effective biosensor based on graphene quantum dots via enzymatic reaction for directly photoluminescence detection of organophosphate pesticide

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Faculty of Science, Chulalongkorn University, Bangkok, 10330, Thailand

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this study, the pesticide sensor by graphene quantum dots (GQDs) and active enzyme, GQDs/enzyme platform, has been developed for monitoring the organophosphate pesticides. In this concept, H2O2 generated from the active enzymatic reaction of acetylcholinesterase (AChE) and choline oxidase (CHOx) enables to react with GQDs resulting in a “turn-off” photoluminescence of GQDs. A “turn-on” photoluminescence of GQDs at 467nm was recovered in the presence of organophosphate. Definitely, the photoluminescence changes of GQDs/AChE/CHOx biosensor reasonably correspond to the amount of pesticide. The detection limit of GQDs/AChE/CHOx biosensor towards dichlorvos was 0.172ppm (0.778μM). In this approach, this biosensor offers the promising determination of the organophosphate pesticides and a benefit for easy checking of organophosphate pesticides in food, water and environment with low cost, easy-to-prepare and less toxic to environment.

ce:section.ce:para: Currently, pesticides are widely used in agricultural products to control or kill unwanted insects, weeds, rodents, fungi, bacteria, or other organisms [1]. The Official of Agricultural Economics of Thailand (OAE) reported that Thailand is tending to increase the importation of pesticides every year, about 149,546 tons in 2015 [2]. The widely used pesticides not only can contaminate in fresh fruits and vegetables but also can generate environmental problems including contamination of air, water, and soil. Even small amount of a highly toxic pesticide will cause serious human health problems [3]. Hence, the United States Environmental Agency (EPA) has set a maximum residue limit for organophosphate pesticide such as dichlorvos at 0.01mg/mL in natural water [1]. Organophosphate pesticides are one type of the pesticide groups. Normally, acetylcholinesterase (AChE) can hydrolyze neurotransmitter acetylcholine to choline and acetate. In the presence of organophosphate compounds, they will covalently bind with an active site of acetylcholinesterase (AChE). This process caused irreversible inhibitor of AChE, leading to the increase of the extremely high level of acetylcholine that causes organ failure and eventual death [4]. Because of high toxicity of pesticides, the sensitive and reliable determination of the organophosphate pesticide residues is of the great importance. Analytical techniques such as GC/MS and LC/MS are usually used to determine the amount of pesticide but these techniques are expensive, time consuming, and require an expertise [5].

In previous reports, the determination of organophosphate pesticides by taking an advantage of inhibition of acetylcholinesterase enzyme were reported with its particular challenges of chemistry for examples of electrochemical method [6–9], fluorescence probe [10–12] and colorimetric detection [13–16]. All of methods are sensitive to detect organophosphate pesticides, but most of the biosensor probes were prepared by a complicated method, highly toxic materials and the use of a large amount of enzymes, leading to high cost. In this work, we attempt to employ an easy and low cost method and a touch on green chemistry in synthesizing the effective photoluminescence GQDs/enzyme biosensor for organophosphate detection.

H2O2 is a well-known oxidizing agent that is widely used for determination not only in organophosphates but also in the important compounds in clinical diagnostics such as glucose. In 2010, Song [17] revealed that the graphene oxide (GO) exhibited the peroxidase-like catalytic activity for the oxidation of 3,3,5,5-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) in the presence of H2O2 for determination of glucose. Works by group of Zheng et al. [18] reported better catalytic oxidation of TMB activity from the smaller sized graphene dots (GDs).

Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) are the graphene sheets smaller than 10–100nm [19]. Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) are currently superior in terms of their excellently attractive properties such as their low toxicity and biocompatibility, high photostability against photobleaching and blinking, as well as small particle size resulting in their unique optical and electronic properties including the quantum confinement, tunable band gap, stable fluorescence, high surface area and edge effect [20–23]. The modified size, surfaces and edges of GQDs lead to the change of band gap and the corresponding photoluminescence that takes an advantage for specific detection of target [24]. Apart from the interesting properties of GQDs, for examples of excellent dispersion and good surface grafting, the GQDs, thus, are of intense current interest in various applications such as bioimaging and biosensor for phenolic compound, biothios and glucose molecule [18,24–26]. Taking on a board the ideas of using peroxidase-mimicking catalyst of graphene materials, the GQDs are attractive candidates for this sensing purpose.

This work explored the detection of organophosphate pesticide by using GQDs as a direct photoluminescence probe via enzymatic reaction. We aim to develop the pesticide sensor by GQDs and active enzyme with BSA, GQDs/enzyme platform, for monitoring the organophosphate pesticides. In this concept as shown in Fig. 1 , H2O2, generated from the active enzymatic reaction of acetylcholinesterase and choline oxidase [15], can react with GQDs, resulting in a “turn-off” fluorescence of GQDs. A “turn-on” fluorescence of GQDs has been recovery in the presence of organophosphate in the system. Definitely, fluorescence changes of GQDs/enzyme biosensor reasonably correspond to the amount of pesticide. In this approach, GQDs/enzyme biosensor is expected to highlight the promising selective determination of the organophosphate pesticides and an attractive material for easy checking of organophosphate pesticides in water with easy-to-prepare and low toxic to environment.

Citric acid monohydrous (C6H8O7·H2O) was purchased from Merck. Acetylcholinesterase (AChE, 217 units/mg) from Electrophorus electricus (electric eel), choline oxidase (CHOx, 14 units/mg) from Alcaligenes sp., neurotransmitter acetylcholine (ACh) and bovine serum albumin (BSA) were purchased from Sigma-aldrich. Organophosphate pesticides including dichlorvos, methyl-paraoxon, malathion and parathion were obtained from Sigma-aldrich. Tris–HCl was purchased from Carlo Erba. Milli-Q (MQ) was prepared by ultra pure water system and used throughout the experiment.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) was performed on a JEOL JEM 2010 with a field emission gun operated at 200kV. The TEM micrographs were used to determine size of the deposited particles by counting approximately 200 particles (ImageJ 1.49V free software with Java 1.6.0_65(32-bit) obtained from https://imagej.nih.gov/ij/, Wayne Rasband, National Institutes of Health, USA). IR spectrophotometric measurement of dried particle samples was performed on Thermo, Nicolet 6700 FT-IR. Absorption spectra were measured by a Varian Cary 50 UV–vis spectrophotometer. Fluorescence spectra were performed on a Varian spectrofluorometer equipped with a personal computer data processing unit. The light source is Cary Eclipse a pulsed xenon lamp and a detector is a photomultiplier tube. Ultra high performance liquid chromatography coupled with mass spectrometry (UHPLC–MS) used in this experiment was performed on Agilent Technologies Model 1290 (CA, USA) including vacuum degasses, binary pump, agilent jet weaver, autosampler and vacumm oven and Agilent Technologies Model 6490 MS (CA, USA) including a triple quadrupoled mass analyzer, electrospray ionization (ESI) interface and MassHunter software processing. A UHPCL separation was performed on an Eclipse XDB-C18 with 4.6mm×50mm, 1.8μm from Agilent (USA).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of the graphene quantum dots
ce:section.ce:para: The preparation of graphene quantum dots (GQDs) was carried out using Dong’s method [20]. Firstly, 2g of citric acid in 5mL beaker was heated to 200°C. After 5min, the solution was turned to orange in 30min. Then the prepared GQDs liquid was added dropwise into 100mL of 10mg/mL−1 NaOH solution under vigorous stirring. After that, the solution was adjusted to pH 8.0 by 1M HCl solution. Then the GQDs were purified by dialysis 2000Da for 15h. The GQDs were solidified by freeze-drying, and then they were weighed and characterized by FTIR spectroscopy and TEM techniques.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of the proposed biosensors and detection of organophosphate
ce:section.ce:para:
The GQDs solution was reasonably utilized to determine the organophosphate pesticide (OP) by following the photoluminescence response of GQDs upon the reaction with H2O2 generated from the Acetylcholinesterase (AChE) and Choline oxidase (CHOx). Each portion of the proposed biosensors were prepared in the same stock solution. Under 40 experiments, 8mL of AChE (10U/mL) and 8mL of CHOx (1.25U/mL) in Tris-BSA (1% of BSA in 50mM pH 8 of Tris buffer) were firstly added into 4mL of GQDs solution (1×10−2 g/mL) and then the solution was added by 16mL of Tris buffer solution (50mM pH 8). The solution was stirred for 5min prior to incubation at 5°C for 24h. Stocks of graphene quantum dots immobilized enzymes (GQDs/AChE/CHOx) were divided into 40 vials with 0.9mL per vial. All vials with GQDs/AChE/CHOx biosensor were kept at 5°C prior to use. Then the solution of 0.7mL of MQ-water and 0.2mL of different concentration of organophosphate pesticide (dissolved in 5% ethanol) was added in the biosensor solution and incubated for 15min in a water bath at 25°C. The mixture solution was added by 0.2mL acetylcholine (10mg/mL) and incubated by stirring for 30min. The PL signals were monitored by fluorescence spectroscopy and the measurements were repeated 3 times. The percentage of enzyme inhibition (I%) was plotted against the concentration of organophosphate pesticide to obtain calibration curve. The percentage of enzyme inhibition (I%) was calculated using Eq. (1) [12], where K30without and K30withOP are the photoluminescence quenching without and with organophosphate pesticide after incubation for 30min.
(1)
I
( % )
=
(
K
30
w i t h o u t
×
K
30
w i t h
O P
K
30
w i t h o u t
)
× 100

The organophosphate detection in real samples was carried out as following: (1) tab water sample, field water samples from Roi Et province and Chulalongkorn University were collected and kept at room temperature for a day. Then, the top layer of water samples was filtrated through a 0.2μm Millipore filter; (the water samples (instead of MQ-water) and the spiked organophosphate pesticide with difference concentration were injected in the GQDs/enzyme biosensor and then the mixture was incubated at 25°C for 15min; (3) the GQDs/enzyme biosensor was added by acetylcholine and then incubated at 25°C for 30min; (4) the PL spectrum was recorded and the PL intensity was plotted as a calibration curve. The organophosphate pesticide in the real sample water was determined from this calibration curve.

A UHPLC–MS method was used to compare the detection affinity of our biosensor. Real samples were prepared according to the procedures from literature [27,28]. The calibration curve was constructed by standard addition of dichlorvos. The UHPLC–MS was completely run within 6min under the flow rate of 0.4mL/min, and injection volume of 2μL. The mobile phases A and B were 0.1% formic acid acetonitrile, respectively. As the first 0.2min, 75% mobile phase A was set with an isocratic run until 3.8min. The electrospray MRM scan mode with positive ion was used to determine the dichlorvos with the transition of the precursor ion of 220.95 to the product ion of 108.9.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Structural morphology characterizations of GQDs and GQDs/AChE/CHOx
ce:section.ce:para: The structural morphologies of graphene quantum dots (GQDs)were elucidated by magnification TEM and TEM images as shown in Fig. 2 A and B, respectively. The GQDs showed spherical shape with average particle size about 5nm (calculated 200 particles from TEM by using the ImageJ software). After incubated in AChE and CHOx in Tris-BSA pH 8.0, the GQDs in GQDs/AChE/CHOx biosensor showed core and light shell (Fig. 2C). Considerably, the average particle size of the core in this platform was slightly increased to 6nm. Approximately particle size of one particle with the entrapment layer was about 20–30nm (yellow arrow). Regarding to the remark increase of particle size, it is possibly caused by a large globular protein of BSA and enzyme coated on GQDs.

The UV–vis spectrum of GQDs displayed an absorption band at 362nm (Fig. 4 A). From the FT-IR and UV results, the GQDs showed abundant sp2 structure and were surrounded by hydroxyl and carboxyl groups. In addition, the GQDs exhibited one strong emission band at 467nm under various excitation wavelength (λex) from 300 to 440nm (Fig. 4B) and showed blue luminescence under UV light (Fig. 4B inset). The excitation-independent photoluminescence of GQDs may attribute to the uniform size and the surface state of sp2 hybridization for GQDs [20]. Apart from a well-defined properties, the excitation-dependent carbon dot based luminescent nanomaterials performed a particular properties of the different size and emission site of each sp2 cluster [32,33].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Role of GQDs toward peroxidase-catalytic reaction of H2O2
ce:section.ce:para:
To verify the reaction time of H2O2 and GQDs, the GQDs solution in the presence of constant amount of H2O2 with various times was investigated. The emission bands at 467nm displayed a large decrease in the range of 0–30min. At time over 30min, the emission bands of GQDs showed a very small decrease. By considering the time efficiency for sensing ability, we selected 30min at the optimum catalytic time of GQDs. Then, we first investigated the peroxidase-like catalytic activity of GQDs by varying the concentration of H2O2. Upon the addition of H2O2, the intensity of PL spectra was decreased as a function of concentration of H2O2 (Fig. 5 A and B). The good linearity in the H2O2 concentration range of 1–125mM with a R2 =0.9849 was shown in Fig. S2. In addition, the Stern-Volmer equation was used to represent the affinity between GQDs and H2O2 quencher as Eq. (2), where the I0 and I are the photoluminescence of GQDs in the absence and presence of H2O2 (ranging from 0 to 50mM) and Ksv is the Stern Volmer constant [34,35]. The Stern-Volmer plots from Fig. S3. showed that the relative photoluminescence intensity of GQDs were depended on the concentration of H2O2 and the Stern-Volmer constant, K sv is 14.04L/mol.
(2)
I
0
I
= 1.0 +
K
S V
[ A n a l y t e s ]

As a well-known aspect, the graphene oxide (GO) and graphene dots (GDs) exhibited the peroxidase-like catalytic activity. The catalytic activity was suggested to occur via the electron transfer process [17,25]. Hence, it was implied that the smaller size of GQDs could easily and directly transfer abundant electron on surface of graphene to LUMO of H2O2 as following Eqs. (3) and (4) , resulting in the PL quenching of the GQDs.
(3) GQDs→GQDs+ +e−
(4) H2O2 +2e− +2H+ →2H2O2 +O2

ce:section.ce:section-title: The GQDs/AChE/CHOx nanomaterial responses to acetylcholine
ce:section.ce:para:
The immobilized enzyme on the materials is reliably important because they take a benefit to encourage the sensitivity and stability of the enzyme [9]. In this work, we expected that the GQDs with the edge of carboxyl and hydroxyl group could easily interact to acetylcholinesterase (AChE) and choline oxidase (CHOx) through hydrogen bonding interaction. To clarify the influence toward fluorescence quenching of GQDs, the factors of AChE and CHOx in the system showed the significant decrease of emission band of GQDs upon increment of time. This phenomenon is analogue to the fluorescence quenching of direct H2O2 added. On the other hand, no such fluorescent changes have been observed for other system without both enzymes of AChE and CHOx incorporated on GQDs as shown in Fig. S4. It has been noticed that the fluorescent quenching of GQDs was occurred by H2O2 generated from the enzymatic reaction as following in Eqs. (5) and (6). Furthermore, the H+ion from dissociation of acetate and electron from GQDs could catalyze turning of H2O2 to water and oxygen as shown in Eqs. (3) and (4), resulting in the observation of small bubble around the reaction vessel.
(5)
a c e t y l c h o l i n e +
H
2
O
→
A C h E
c h o l i n e + a c e t a t e
(6)
c h o l i n e +
O
2
→
C H O x
H
2
O
2

The time-dependent enzymatic reaction relied on GQDs/AChE/CHOx biosensor in the presence of acetylcholine was investigated in Tris buffer pH 8 as shown in Fig. 6 A. Decreasing the emission band at 467nm was related to the amount of H2O2 generated by enzymatic reaction as a function of the reaction time. To verify the proper amount of enzyme, the different amount of AChE and CHOx in GQDs/AChE/CHOx biosensor under varying time was examined in Tris buffer solution pH 8.0. As seen in Fig. 6B, the fluorescence intensity at 467nm was significantly decreased upon a particular amount of AChE at 1U and CHOx at 0.125U and 0.25U under the reaction of time of 60min (the yellow and green line in Fig. 6B).

Taking on a board of the material cost used, the optimum requirement for employing the amount of enzyme in this study was selected as 1U/mL AChE and 0.125U/mL CHOx which were much lower than the previous works based on this enzymatic reaction [12,14,15]. The incubation time was investigated from 0 to 60min after addition of acetylcholine. The normalized PL intensity at 467nm was completely decreased upon the incubation time of 60min (red dotted line of Fig. 6B). However, the incubation time of 60 min has not been effective for real time detection test. Deeply considering, the incubation time at 30min of the GQDs in the presence of 1U/mL AChE and 0.125U/mL CHOx exhibited over 50% quenching of emission band and then the incubation time over 30 min displayed a small quenching of emission band. Thus, the incubation time of the GQDs/AChE/CHOx biosensor with acetylcholine at 30min was chosen for all further manipulation.

The pH effect on this biosensor is a crucial role for this analysis. Upon the pH increasing, the PL intensity of GQDs/AChE/CHOx biosensor (Fig. S5) performed the increasing tendency until pH 8–9. At pH>9, the maximum emission band were slightly decreased. Furthermore, the PL behavior in the presence of ACh with various pH was examined in the range of pH 5–9. As seen in Fig. S6, a large decrease of emission band at 467nm was observed especially pH 7–9. Actually, it was well-known that, most organophosphates were moderately unstable at high temperature and high pH (for example, half-life hydrolysis of dichlorvos at 25°C at pH 6.0, 7.0 and 8.0 were 46, 2.8days and 0.91 days, respectively) [36,37]. Additionally, previous researches reported the highest activity of free AChE and CHOx enzymes at pH 8 [38–40]. As reasonable consistency with above, a similar fluorescent quenching of GQDs/AChE/CHOx biosensor at pH 7–9 pursued us to study the organophosphate detection at pH 8 for all manipulation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: OPs detection by using the GQDs/AChE/CHOx nanomaterial
ce:section.ce:para: The PL spectra of this biosensor toward dichlorvos were shown in Fig. 7 A. The PL intensity of GQDs was significantly increased upon the increment of dichlorvos. This indicated that organophosphate pesticides (OPs) actually inhibited the activity of enzyme acetylcholinesterase (AChE) and led to the decrease of H2O2 resulting in turn-on PL intensity of GQDs in the presence of OPs. Collectively, these results demonstrated that GQDs/AChE/CHOx biosensor can assemble onto indirect detection of the amount of OPs through the inhibition of AChE enzymatic reaction, thereby inducing a fluorescence recovery.

Four selected organophosphates (OPs)including methy-paraoxon, dichlorvos, malathion and parathion at concentration of 1ppm and 10ppm were further examined in a regard to an inhibition efficiency of AChE enzymatic reaction as shown Fig. 7B. Of particular concentration of OPs at 1ppm addressed the%inhibition of methy-paraoxon, dichlorvos, malathion and parathion at 62±5%, 30±2%, 7±3% and 6±1% respectively. However, the%inhibition efficiency in both concentrations of OPs showed the similar tendency change for all OPs.Taking such a previous work in the inhibition capacity of OPs toward AChE, a possible effect of the electrophilicity at a phosphorus atom has been reliably considered since the inhibition capacity of the oxo-forms is more efficient than the thio-form [8,11,16]. Another factor is steric effect of OPs to the active center of AChE containing a deep and narrow gorge [12,41]. In these regards, the inhibition capacity of dichlorvos and methyl-paraoxon is more efficient than that of malathion andparathion.Concerning on the different inhibition capacity of OPs, it was a possible attribution of the reciprocal absorption capacity of OPs on the nanomaterial surface. According to the chemical structure of OPs, dichlorvos pesticide has more hydrophobic structure than methyl-paraoxon.This was possibly rationalized that the hydrophobic dichlorvos did not prefer passing through hydrophilic phase of GQDs/AChE/CHOx biosensor resulting in a lower inhibition capacity [12]. Such the inhibition of dichlorvos and methyl-paraoxon has been extensively studied in sensing applications.

The% inhibition efficiency (I%) of enzyme was plotted toward the concentration of dichlorvos and methyl-paraoxon as shown in Fig. 8 A and B. The suitably linear range of 0.1–10ppm for dichlorvos (inset Fig. 8A) showed the% inhibition efficiency (I%) of 27.173+22.488log[dichlorvos], R2 =0.99 and n=3. Normally, the detection limit of pesticide by sensory system is acceptable at 10% inhibition based on AChE activity [41]. Consequently, detection limit of dichlorvos by GQDs/AChE/CHOx biosensor was 0.172ppm (0.778μM). In comparison, this value is lower than a potentiometric detection [6], the enzymetic immobilization on working electrode for an amperometric detection [7], the luminescence quenching probe of Tb3+ complex [42] and an optical mode [43] as shown in Table 1 . Interestingly, the PL changes of GQDs/AChE/CHOx biosensor are covered in the range of a maximum residue limit of dichlorvos at 10ppm in natural water authorized by the United States Environmental Agency (EPA) [1]. In the case of methyl-paraoxon, the linear range of 0.1–1ppm (0.40–4.05μM) (inset 8B) with the equation of I%=55.253+42.158log[methy-paraoxon], R2 =0.99 and n=3 demonstrated the detection limit of 0.084ppm (0.342μM), this value is lower than the spectrophotometric cutinized assay [44] and conductometric detection [45] as shown in Table 1. Although this sensing platform provided an excellent analytical LOD for methyl-paraoxon over dichlorvos, a dramatic increase of dichlorvos usage in agriculture in Thailand [46] urged us to focus on studying dichlorvos sensing purpose by this sensing platform.

Extensive work for visual determination of this biosensor toward dichlorvos was carried out under visible and UV light. The naked-eye brightness upon the different concentration of dichlorvos at 1, 10 and 100ppm with and without ACh under UV light has been exhibited in Fig. 9 . Under the UV light, the sensing platform showed strong blue brightness (right tube of Fig. 9B) and a non-bright solution was observed after adding ACh in the absence of OP, (left tube of Fig. 9B). For the remaining solution in the middle tubes of Fig. 9B, the tendency of blue brightness of this sensing platform has been raised in regarding to an increment of dichlorvos concentration. These results suggested that GQDs/AChE/CHOx biosensor was capable of detecting dichlorvos by quantitative visual interpretation.

Due to the high abundance of metal ions in soil and water [42] the interference of metal ions for the OPs determination by this system was investigated. Fig. 10 showed the inhibition efficiency of AChE in the sensing platform without (blackbar) and with (red bar)the interferences (1×10−5 M) upon incubation with dichlorvos at the concentration of 2ppm (1×10−5 M). The results showed that most metal ions exhibited no interference to the dichlorvos detection. However, the GQDs/AChE/CHOx biosensor exhibited a small quenching of PL response in the presence of Hg2+. The previous work related to the GQDs optical properties also reported the influence of Hg2+ toward the fluorescence quenching of GQDs [47,48]. To test the effect of Hg2+ toward our sensor system, the free GQDs without enzymatic reaction has been further examined by adding the Hg2+ in the solution.It was found that Hg2+ affected directly to the PL quenching of GQDs as shown in Fig. S7.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of organophosphate in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: In previous works, the determination of organophosphate has been carried out in MQ-water or DI water [15,42]. Additionally, the LC–MS method required the complicated sample pretreatment to elude some interference [28,49,50]. Hence, this method showed the advantage of direct detection of dichlorvos in natural water under a small effect of interference. In order to determine dichlorvos in real samples, three samples of water (tab water from the laboratory, field water 1 from the lake at Chulalongkorn University and field water 2 from the lake at Roi Et province near agricultural land)were studied in this work.The water was collected a day prior to the experiment. The calibration curve of dichlorvos was achieved from the standard addition experiment. Table 2 demonstrates the correlation of concentration of organophosphate that was determined by GQDs/AChE/CHOx biosensor and LC/MS method. According to the acceptable recovery of 80–110% for the analyte concentrations at 0.1–10ppm, and their acceptable relative standard deviation (RSD) of 14% at 2.0ppm [51], satisfactory accuracy in the amount of dichlorvos at 2.0ppm spiked in the solution system was obtained with the recovery of 101–112% and 91.5–92.0% for the GQDs/AChE/CHOx biosensor and UHPLC–MS/MS, respectively, along with the satisfactory precision with%RSD of 2.3–5.9% and 0.15–0.16% for the GQDs/AChE/CHOx biosensor and UHPLC–MS/MS, respectively. This indicates that, in comparison with the UHPLC–MS/MS method, our developed GQDs/AChE/CHOx biosensor can be used with the acceptable accuracy and precision in quantitative analysis of dichlorvos in various water samples.

In summary, we have successfully demonstrated the real-time sensor of graphene quantum dots based enzymatic reaction (GQDs/AChE/CHOx) for adaptive determination ability toward organophosphate pesticides (OPs). Taking an advantage, GQDs/AChE/CHOx biosensor would be easily oxidized by H2O2 at pH 8 condition inducing the fluorescence quenching. Upon the addition of dichlorvos and methyl-paraoxon, it will react with acetylcholinesterase (AChE) to inhibit the generation of H2O2 in the system resulting in a strong fluorescence response. This biosensor highlighted sensitive and rapid detection of dichlorvos with detection limit of 0.172ppm (0.778μM) that was lower than the previously published method. Additionally, this approach offered the benefit for determination of the dichlorvos in real samples. Moreover, this proposed biosensor is low cost, using non-toxic materials, unnecessary sample pretreatment, and is convenient to use without expensive, unstable dye.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Authors would like to acknowledge the Ratchadapisek Sompoch Endowment Fund (2016), Chulalongkorn University (CU-59-020-FW) and the 90th Anniversary of Chulalongkorn Fund (Ratchadapisek Sompoch Endowment Fund) for research grant. CS is the Ph.D student supported by the Development and Promotion of Science and Technology Talent Projects (DPST).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2017.11.072.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:

ce:other-ref.ce:textref: The Official of Agricultural Economic of Thailand (OAE), The Official of Agricultural Economic of Thailand (OAE) 2015.
The United States Department of Agriculture (USDA), The pesticide data program annual summary calendaryear 2005, Washington, DC, 2005.

Economical, green synthesis of fluorescent carbon nanoparticles and their use as probes for sensitive and selective detection of mercury(II) ions

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Chonticha Sahub is currently Ph.D. student at Department of Chemistry, Faculty of Science, Chulalongkorn University under supervision of Assistant Professor Dr. Boosayarat Tomapatanaget. She has obtained the scholarship from the Development and Promotion of Science and Technology Talent Project (DPST), under the Institute for the Promotion of Teaching Science and Technology (IPST), Thailand since 2003. She received the DPST fund for joint research with Professor Vincent M. Rotello at University of Massachusetts Amherst, USA in 2012 and with Professor Jonathan W. Steed at Durham University, UK in 2017. Her research interest include nanotechnology for drug delivery and sensing application.

Thawatchai Tuntulani is currently a professor in the Department of Chemistry at Chulalongkorn University, Thailand. He earned his PhD in Chemistry with Prof. Marcetta Y. Darensbourg at Texas A&M University, USA after obtaining a Bachelor Degree of Engineering from Chiang Mai University, Thailand. He did his postdoctoral studies with Prof. Jean-Marie Lehn before joining Chulalongkorn University in 1995. His research interests include syntheses of derivatives of calix[4]arene and other macrocyclic compounds and development of electrochemical and optical sensing materials for ions and biomolecules.

Thumnoon Nhujak is currently an associate professor in the Department of Chemistry, Faculty of Science, Chulalongkorn University, Thailand. He received his PhD in Chemistry from University of York, UK in 2001, under supervision of Professor David M. Goodall. His current research interests focus on separation techniques including capillary electrophoresis, liquid chromatography and liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry.

Boosayarat Tomapatanaget is currently an assistant professor Department of Chemistry, Faculty of Science, Chulalongkorn University. She received her B.S. (1995) and Ph.D. (2003) degrees in Chulalongkorn University under supervision of Professor Thawatchai Tuntulani. She received the Royal Golden Jubilee Scholarship from the Thailand Research Fund to do research under supervision of Professor. Paul D. Beer at the University of Oxford, UK. Her research interest is host-guest chemistry including synthesis of a new sensory molecule, nanotechnology for sensing application by using the fluorescence, UV–vis and electrochemical techniques for detection of anions, cations and neutral molecules.
